Title: A version control tool is used to manage the codebase (e.g., Git)
Argo Data accessed locally by the software are assumed to be organised following the GDAC folder structure
Argo Data accessed remotely by the software are retrieved from an official GDAC server (http, ftp, s3 servers)
Argo Data used by the software are traceable with a persistent identifier (Argo GDAC DOI or snapshot DOIs)
Argo Meta-Data accessed remotely by the software are retrieved from an official Argo sources (GDAC ftp, GDAC https, NVS, SparQL, STAC)
Argo papers and DOIs are referenced in the software metadata
Code review meetings are organized when needed
Data produced by the software preserve the Argo license (CC BY 4.0)
Data produced by the software respect Argo file formats and conventions wherever relevant (e.g. NetCDF CF-1.7)
Each collaborator is clearly identified within the project
Each pull request is reviewed by at least one collaborator
Every significant change within the code is managed through a pull (or merge) request
Issues or bug reports are described as fully as possible (e.g. use labels, reproducible example)
Issues or bugs are explicitly referencing the concerned Argo mission (e.g. core, deep, BGC, ...) or component (e.g. trajectories, decoding,...)
Issues or bugs are managed through the web-based development platform (e.g., GitHub or GitLab issues)
Metadata produced by the software follow Argo conventions (as in reference tables provided by the NVS server)
Non-Argo Data used by the software are explicitly identified and traceable with a persistent identifier (DOI) when available
Non-Argo Data used by the software should be accessible online
Project meetings are periodically organized
Software training sessions are periodically organized
The codebase includes comments and is documented following referenced standards (e.g. numpy style docstrings in Python)
The codebase includes comments and is documented to explain the purpose and functionality of each module, class, and function
The codebase is "consistent", i.e. it follows consistent naming conventions, coding styles, and design patterns throughout the software
The codebase is designed with modular components to facilitate reuse, testing and maintenance
The codebase is formatted following referenced standards (e.g. black in Python)
The methodology implemented by the software has been presented to the Argo community (e.g. Data Management Team meeting)
The project allows any user to submit an issue or bug report and ease the process (e.g. provide list of labels, issue templates)
The project clearly list all software identifiers in the README file (e.g. DOI, SWHID)
The project defines expected standards of conduct when engaging in the project (e.g. includes a CODE_OF_CONDUCT file to the codebase)
The project has multiple collaborators from outside the Argo community
The project has multiple collaborators from the Argo community
The project include a CITATION.cff file to indicate how to cite the software
The project includes a CONTRIBUTING file
The project includes a LICENSE file
The project includes a README file
The project includes a basic description of what it does with or to the Argo dataset (e.g. read index file, calibrate salinity, plot trajectory, assess BGC variables,...)
The project includes a basic documentation (e.g. a README file)
The project includes a change log
The project includes a document describing expected contributions (e.g. a CONTRIBUTING file)
The project is hosted on a web-based development platform (e.g., GitHub or GitLab).
The project is hosted on an Argo web-based development platform (eg: github/euroargodev, github/ArgoDMQC, github/ArgoRTQC or any other project or institute level organisation)
The software documentation and codebase are written in English
The software documentation has an API reference section automatically generated
The software documentation includes installation guidelines
The software documentation includes the definition of the execution environment supported
The software documentation includes the list of changes to the codebase between each software releases
The software documentation includes the list of operating systems supported
The software documentation includes usage guidelines and examples
The software documentation is hosted on the web
The software ensures timely distribution of updates
The software has a unique persistent identifier (e.g. DOI from paper or Zenodo, SWHID)
The software has been presented to the Argo community (e.g. Data Management Team meeting)
The software includes continuous integration and unit testing
The software is distributed via well-known services (e.g., pip or conda for Python)
The software is registered in an Argo software registry (e.g. AST and Argo-BGC dedicated webpages)
The software is registered in at least one public software registry (e.g., Zenodo, Seanoe, Research Software Directory)
The software is released under an open-source license (include an open-source LICENSE file with the license content to the codebase)
The software provides a clear description of its dependencies
The software uses a programming language adopted within the Argo community (e.g. Matlab, Python, R, Java, Julia)
The software uses an open-source programming language (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_open-source_programming_languages)
The software uses the Argo vocabulary server (to enforce conventions as defined in Argo reference tables)
There is a published paper associated with the software (e.g. JOSS article)
Unit tests cover multiple operating systems
User meetings or workshops are held and communicated within the Argo community
